english_head: if a review of our argument discloses any new point of view? By all means. We say, then, that if the one exists, we must come to an agreement about the consequences, whatever they may be, do we not? Yes. Now consider the first point. If one is, can it be and not partake of being? No, it cannot. Then the being of one will exist, but will not be identical with one; for if it were identical with one, it would not be the being of one, nor would one partake of it,

greek_head: ἐκεῖνο τὸ ἕν ἐκείνης μετεῖχεν ἀλλ' ὅμοιον ἂν ἦν λέγειν ἕν τε εἶναι καὶ ἓν ἕν νῦν δὲ οὐχ αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ ὑπόθεσις
greek_tokens: ekeinos o eis ekeinos metexw alla omoios an eimi legw eis te eimi kai eis eis nun de ou outos eimi o upoqesis ei eis eis tis xrh sumbainw alla ei eis eimi ou outws panu men oun oukoun ws allos tis shmainw o eimi o eis anagkh ara oun allos h ostis ousia metexw o eis outos an eaw o legw epeidan tis sullhbdhn eipon ostis eis eimi panu ge palin dh legw eis ei eimi tis sumbainw skopaw oun ei ou anagkh outos o upoqesis toioutos eimi o eis shmainw oios
english_head: but the statement that one is would be equivalent to the statement that one is one but our hypothesis is not if one is one, what will follow, but if one is. Do you agree? Certainly. In the belief that one and being differ in meaning? Most assuredly. Then if we say concisely one is, it is equivalent to saying that one partakes of being? Certainly. Let us again say what will follow if one is and consider whether this hypothesis must not necessarily show that one is of such a nature as to have parts. How does that come about ? In this way:

greek_head: μέρη ἔχειν Πῶς Ὧδε εἰ τὸ ἔστι τοῦ ἑνὸς ὄντος λέγεται καὶ τὸ ἓν τοῦ ὄντος ἑνός ἔστι δὲ οὐ τὸ αὐτὸ ἥ τε
greek_tokens: mera exw pws wde ei o eimi o eis eimi legw kai o eis o eimi eis eimi de ou o autos o te ousia kai o eis o autos de ekeinos e upotiqhmi o eis eimi ara ou anagkh o men oloc eis eimi eimi autos outos de gignomai morion o te eis kai o eimi anagkh poteros oun ekateros o morion outos morion monos proserew h o oloc morion o ge morion prosrhteos o oloc kai oloc ara eimi o an eis eimi kai morion exis panu ge tis oun o morion ekateros
english_head: If being is predicated of the one which exists and unity is predicated of being which is one, and being and the one are not the same, but belong to the existent one of our hypothesis, must not the existent one be a whole of which the one and being are parts? Inevitably. And shall we call each of these parts merely a part, or must it, in so far as it is a part, be called a part of the whole? A part of the whole. Whatever one, then, exists is a whole and has a part. Certainly. Well then, can either of these two parts of existent one—unity and being—abandon the other?

greek_head: τούτων τοῦ ἑνὸς ὄντος τό τε ἓν καὶ τὸ ὄν ἆρα ἀπολείπεσθον ἢ τὸ ἓν τοῦ εἶναι μορίου ἢ τὸ ὂν τοῦ ἑνὸς μορίου Οὐκ
greek_tokens: outos o eis eimi o te eis kai o eimi ara apoleipw h o eis o eimi morion h o eimi o eis morion ou an eaw palin ara kai o morion ekateros o te eis isxw kai o eimi kai gignomai o elaxistos ek duo au morion o morion kai kata o autos logos outws aei oti an morion gignomai outos o morion aei isxw o te gar eis o eimi aei isxw kai o eimi o eis wste anagkh
english_head: Can unity cease to be a part of being or being to be a part of unity? No. And again each of the parts possesses unity and being, and the smallest of parts is composed of these two parts, and thus by the same argument any part whatsoever has always these two parts; for always unity has being and being has unity;

greek_head: δύ' ἀεὶ γιγνόμενον μηδέποτε ἓν εἶναι Παντάπασι μὲν οὖν Οὐκοῦν ἄπειρον ἂν τὸ πλῆθος οὕτω τὸ ἓν ὂν εἴη
greek_tokens: duh aei gignomai mhdepote eis eimi pantapasi men oun oukoun apeiros an o plhqos outws o eis eimi eaw eoika eimi dh kai ode eti ph ousia fhmi metexw o eis dio eimi nai kai dia outos dh o eis eimi polus fainw outws tis de autos o eis o dh fhmi ousia metexw ean autos o dianoia monos kaqa eautou lambanw aneu outos e fhmi metexw ara ge eis monos fainw h kai polus o autos outos eis oimh egw
english_head: and, therefore, since it is always becoming two, it can never be one. Certainly. Then it results that the existent one would be infinite in number? Apparently. Let us make another fresh start. In what direction? We say that the one partakes of being, because it is? Yes. And for that reason the one, because it is, was found to be many. Yes. Well then, will the one, which we say partakes of being, if we form a mental conception of it alone by itself, without that of which we say it partakes, be found to be only one, or many? One, I should say.

greek_head: Ἴδωμεν δή ἄλλο τι ἕτερον μὲν ἀνάγκη τὴν οὐσίαν αὐτοῦ εἶναι ἕτερον δὲ αὐτό εἴπερ μὴ οὐσία τὸ ἕν ἀλλ' ὡς ἓν
greek_tokens: eidon dh allos tis eteros men anagkh o ousia autos eimi eteros de autos eiper mh ousia o eis alla ws eis ousia metexw anagkh oukoun ei eteros men o ousia eteros de o eis oute o eis o eis o ousia eteros oute o ousia eimi o ousia o eis allos alla o eteros te kai allos eteros allhlwn panu men oun wste ou tautos eimi oute o eis oute o ousia o eteros pws gar
english_head: Just let us see; must not the being of one be one thing and one itself another, if the one is not being, but, considered as one, partakes of being? Yes, that must be so. Then if being is one thing and one is another, one is not other than being because it is one, nor is being other than one because it is being, but they differ from each other by virtue of being other and different. Certainly. Therefore the other is neither the same as one nor as being. Certainly not. Well, then, if we make a selection among them,

greek_head: Τί οὖν ἐὰν προελώμεθα αὐτῶν εἴτε βούλει τὴν οὐσίαν καὶ τὸ ἕτερον εἴτε τὴν οὐσίαν καὶ τὸ ἓν εἴτε τὸ ἓν καὶ τὸ
greek_tokens: tis oun ean proairew autos eite boulomai o ousia kai o eteros eite o ousia kai o eis eite o eis kai o eteros ara ou en ekastos o proairesis proairew tis os orqos exis kalew amfoteros pws wde eimi ousia eipon eimi kai auqis eipon eis kai outos ara oun ou ekateros autos eromai nai tis de otan eipon ousia te kai eis ara ou amfoteros panu ge oukoun kai ean ousia te kai eteros h eteros te kai eis kai outws pantaxws epi ekastos amfw legos nai
english_head: whether we select being and the other, or being and one, or one and the other, in each instance we select two things which may properly be called both? What do you mean? I will explain. We can speak of being? Yes. And we can also speak of one? Yes, that too. Then have we not spoken of each of them? Yes. And when I speak of being and one, do I not speak of both? Certainly. And also when I speak of being and other, or other and one, in every case I speak of each pair as both?

greek_head: Ὣ δ' ἂν ἄμφω ὀρθῶς προσαγορεύησθον ἆρα οἷόν τε ἄμφω μὲν αὐτὼ εἶναι δύο δὲ μή Οὐχ οἷόν τε Ὣ δ' ἂν δύο
greek_tokens: os de an amfw orqos prosagoreuw ara oios te amfw men autos eimi duo de mh ou oios te os de an duo eimi eimi tis mhxanh mh ou ekateros autos eis eimi oudeis outos ara epei sunduo ekastos sumbainw eimi kai eis an eaw ekastos fainw ei de eis ekastos autos eimi suntiqhmi eis opoios ostis suzugios ou treis gignomai o pas nai treis de ou perissos kai duo artios pws de ou tis de duo eimi ou
english_head: Yes. If things are correctly called both, can they be both without being two? They cannot. And if things are two, must not each of them be one? Certainly. Then since the units of these pairs are together two, each must be individually one. That is clear. But if each of them is one, by the addition of any sort of one to any pair whatsoever the total becomes three? Yes. And three is an odd number, and two is even? Of course.

greek_head: ἀνάγκη εἶναι καὶ δίς καὶ τριῶν ὄντων τρίς εἴπερ ὑπάρχει τῷ τε δύο τὸ δὶς ἓν καὶ τῷ τρία τὸ τρὶς ἕν Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: anagkh eimi kai dis kai treis eimi tris eiper uparxw o te duo o dis eis kai o treis o tris eis anagkh duo de eimi kai dis ou anagkh duo dis eimi kai treis kai tris ou anagkh au treis tris eimi pws de ou tis de treis eimi kai dis eimi kai duo eimi kai tris eimi ou anagkh te treis dis eimi kai duo tris polus ge artios te ara artiakis an eaw kai
english_head: Well, when there are two units, must there not also be twice, and when there are three, thrice, that is, if two is twice one and three is thrice one? There must. But if there are two and twice, must there not also be twice two? And again, if there are three and thrice, must there not be thrice three? Of course. Well then, if there are three and twice and two and thrice, must there not also be twice three and thrice two? Inevitably. Then there would be even times even,

greek_head: περιττὰ περιττάκις καὶ ἄρτια περιττάκις καὶ περιττὰ ἀρτιάκις Ἔστιν οὕτω Εἰ οὖν ταῦτα οὕτως ἔχει οἴει τινὰ
greek_tokens: perissos perittakis kai artios perittakis kai perissos artiakis eimi outws ei oun outos outws exis oiomai tis ariqmos upoleipw os ou anagkh eimi oudamos ge ei ara eimi eis anagkh kai ariqmos eimi anagkh alla meis ariqmew ge eimi polus an eaw kai plhqos apeiros o eimi h ou apeiros ariqmos plhqw kai metexw ousia gignomai kai panu ge oukoun ei pas ariqmos ousia metexw kai o morion ekastos o ariqmew metexw an autos nai
english_head: odd times odd, odd times even, and even times odd. Yes. Then if that is true, do you believe any number is left out, which does not necessarily exist? By no means. Then if one exists, number must also exist. It must. But if number exists, there must be many, indeed an infinite multitude, of existences; or is not number infinite in multitude and participant of existence? Certainly it is. Then if all number partakes of existence, every part of number will partake of it? Yes.

greek_head: Ἐπὶ πάντα ἄρα πολλὰ ὄντα ἡ οὐσία νενέμηται καὶ οὐδενὸς ἀποστατεῖ τῶν ὄντων οὔτε τοῦ σμικροτάτου οὔτε
greek_tokens: epi pas ara polus eimi o ousia nemw kai oudeis apostatew o eimi oute o mikros oute o megas h outos men kai alogos eromai pws gar an dh ousia ge o eimi tis apostatew oudamos katakermatizw ara ws oios te mikros kai megas kai pantaxws eimi kai merizw pas malista kai eimi
english_head: Existence, then, is distributed over all things, which are many, and is not wanting in any existing thing from the greatest to the smallest? Indeed, is it not absurd even to ask that question? For how can existence be wanting in any existing thing? It cannot by any means. Then it is split up into the smallest and greatest and all kinds of existences nothing else is so much divided,

greek_head: μέρη ἀπέραντα τῆς οὐσίας Ἔχει οὕτω Πλεῖστα ἄρα ἐστὶ τὰ μέρη αὐτῆς Πλεῖστα μέντοι Τί οὖν ἔστι τι αὐτῶν ὃ
greek_tokens: mera aperantos o ousia exis outws pleistos ara eimi o mera autos pleistos mentoi tis oun eimi tis autos o eimi men meros o ousia oudeis mentoi meros kai pws an su outos gignomai alla eiper ge oimh eimi anagkh autos aei ewsper an eimi eis ge tis eimi mhdeis de adunatos anagkh pros apas ara ekastos o o ousia meros proseimi o eis ou apoleipw oute mikros oute megas meros oute allos oudeis outws ara oun eis
english_head: and in short the parts of existence are infinite. That is true. Its parts are the most numerous of all. Yes, they are the most numerous. Well, is there any one of them which is a part of existence, but is no part? How could that be? But if there is, it must, I imagine, so long as it is, be some one thing; it cannot be nothing. That is inevitable. Then unity is an attribute of every part of existence and is not wanting to a smaller or larger or any other part. True.

greek_head: ὂν πολλαχοῦ ἅμα ὅλον ἐστί τοῦτο ἄθρει Ἀλλ' ἀθρῶ καὶ ὁρῶ ὅτι ἀδύνατον Μεμερισμένον ἄρα εἴπερ μὴ ὅλον
greek_tokens: eimi pollaxou ama oloc eimi outos aqrew alla aqrew kai oraw ostis adunatos merizw ara eiper mh oloc allos gar pou oudamos ama apas o o ousia meros pareimi h merizw nai kai meis o ge meristos polus anagkh eimi tosoutos osos mera anagkh ou ara alhqhs arti legw legw ws pleistos mera o ousia nemw eaw oude gar plew o eis nemw alla
english_head: Can the one be in many places at once and still be a whole? Consider that question. I am considering and I see that it is impossible. Then it is divided into parts, if it is not a whole; for it cannot be attached to all the parts of existence at once unless it is divided. I agree. And that which is divided into parts must certainly be as numerous as its parts. It must. Then what we said just now—that existence was divided into the greatest number of parts—was not true for it is not divided, you see, into any more parts than one,

greek_head: ἴσα ὡς ἔοικε τῷ ἑνί οὔτε γὰρ τὸ ὂν τοῦ ἑνὸς ἀπολείπεται οὔτε τὸ ἓν τοῦ ὄντος ἀλλ' ἐξισοῦσθον δύο ὄντε ἀεὶ παρὰ
greek_tokens: isos ws eoika o eis oute gar o eimi o eis apoleipw oute o eis o eimi alla ecisow duo eimi aei para pas pantapasi outws fainw o eis ara autos kermatizw upo o ousia polus te kai apeiros o plhqos eimi fainw ou monos ara o eimi eis polus eimi alla kai autos o eis upo o eimi dianemw polus anagkh eimi pantapasi men oun kai meis ostis ge oloc o morion morion perainw an eaw kata o oloc o eis h ou periexw upo o oloc o
english_head: but, as it seems, into the same number as one for existence is not wanting to the one, nor the one to existence, but being two they are equal throughout. That is perfectly clear. The one, then, split up by existence, is many and infinite in number. Clearly. Then not only the existent one is many, but the absolute one divided by existence, must be many. Certainly. And because the parts are parts of a whole, the one would be limited by the whole;

greek_head: μόρια Ἀνάγκη Ἀλλὰ μὴν τό γε περιέχον πέρας ἂν εἴη Πῶς δ' οὔ Τὸ ἓν ἄρα ὂν ἕν τέ ἐστί που καὶ πολλά καὶ
greek_tokens: morion anagkh alla meis o ge periexw pera an eaw pws de ou o eis ara eimi eis su eimi pou kai polus kai oloc kai morion kai perainw kai apeiros plhqw fainw ara oun ou epei perainw kai esxatos exw anagkh tis de ei oloc ou kai arxh an exw kai mesos kai teleuth h oios su tis oloc eimi aneu treis outos kan tis eis otioun autos apostatew eqelw eti oloc eimi ou eqelw kai arxh dh ws eoika kai
english_head: or are not the parts included by the whole? They must be so. But surely that which includes is a limit. Of course. Then the existent one is, apparently, both one and many, a whole and parts, limited and of infinite number. So it appears. Then if limited it has also extremes ? Certainly. Yes, and if it is a whole, will it not have a beginning, a middle, and an end? Or can there be any whole without these three? And if any one of these is wanting, will it still be a whole? It will not.

greek_head: τελευτὴν καὶ μέσον ἔχοι ἂν τὸ ἕν Ἔχοι Ἀλλὰ μὴν τό γε μέσον ἴσον τῶν ἐσχάτων ἀπέχει οὐ γὰρ ἂν ἄλλως μέσον
greek_tokens: teleuth kai mesos exw an o eis exw alla meis o ge mesos isos o esxatos apexw ou gar an allos mesos eaw ou gar kai sxhma dh tis ws eoika toioutos eimi metexw an o eis htoi euqhs h stroggulos h tis miktos ek amfw metexw gar an ara oun outws exw ou autos te en eautou eimi kai en allos pws o mera pou ekastos en o oloc eimi kai oudeis ektos o oloc outws pas de o mera upo o
english_head: Then the one, it appears, will have a beginning, a middle, and an end. It will. But surely the middle is equally distant from the extremes for otherwise it would not be a middle. No. And the one, apparently, being of such a nature, will partake of some shape, whether straight or round or a mixture of the two. Yes, it will. This being the case, will not the one be in itself and in other? How is that? Each of the parts doubtless is in the whole and none is outside of the whole. True. And all the parts are included in the whole ?

greek_head: ὅλου περιέχεται Ναί Καὶ μὴν τά γε πάντα μέρη τὰ αὑτοῦ τὸ ἕν ἐστι καὶ οὔτε τι πλέον οὔτε ἔλαττον ἢ πάντα
greek_tokens: oloc periexw nai kai meis o ge pas mera o eautou o eis eimi kai oute tis pleos oute elasswn h pas ou gar oukoun kai o oloc o eis eimi pws de ou ei ara pas o mera en oloc tugxanw eimi eimi de o te pas o eis kai autos o oloc periexw de upo o oloc o pas upo o eis an periexw o eis kai outws an hdh o eis autos en eautou eaw fainw alla mentoi o ge oloc au ou en o meros eimi oute en pas oute
english_head: Yes. And surely the one is all its parts, neither more nor less than all. Certainly. But the whole is the one, is it not? Of course. Then if all the parts are in the whole and all the parts are the one and the one is also the whole, and all the parts are included in the whole, the one will be included in the one, and thus the one will be in itself. Evidently. But the whole is not in the parts, neither in all of them nor in any.

greek_head: ἐν τινί εἰ γὰρ ἐν πᾶσιν ἀνάγκη καὶ ἐν ἑνί ἔν τινι γὰρ ἑνὶ μὴ ὂν οὐκ ἂν ἔτι που δύναιτο ἔν γε ἅπασιν εἶναι εἰ δὲ
greek_tokens: en tis ei gar en pas anagkh kai en eis en tis gar eis mh eimi ou an eti pou dunamai en ge apas eimi ei de outos men o eis o apas eimi o de oloc en outos mh en pws eti en ge o pas eneimi oudamos oude meis en tis o mera ei gar en tis o oloc eaw o pleos an en o elasswn eaw o eimi adunatos adunatos gar mh eimi de en pleiwn mhde en eis mhde en apas o meros o oloc ou anagkh en eteros tis eimi h mhdamos
english_head: For if it is in all, it must be in one, for if it were wanting in any one it could no longer be in all; for if this one is one of all, and the whole is not in this one, how can it still be in all? It cannot in any way. Nor can it be in some of the parts; for if the whole were in some parts, the greater would be in the less, which is impossible. Yes, it is impossible. But not being in one or several or all of the parts, it must be in something else or cease to be anywhere at all? It must. And if it were nowhere, it would be nothing, but being a whole, since it is not in itself, it must be in something else, must it not?

greek_head: ἔτι εἶναι Ἀνάγκη Οὐκοῦν μηδαμοῦ μὲν ὂν οὐδὲν ἂν εἴη ὅλον δὲ ὄν ἐπειδὴ οὐκ ἐν αὑτῷ ἐστιν ἀνάγκη ἐν ἄλλῳ
greek_tokens: eti eimi anagkh oukoun mhdamos men eimi oudeis an eaw oloc de eimi epei ou en eautou eimi anagkh en allos eimi panu ge h men ara o eis oloc en allos eimi h de o pas mera eimi tugxanw autos en eautou kai outws o eis anagkh autos te en eautou eimi kai en eteros anagkh outws dh fuw o eis ara ou anagkh kai kinew kai isthmi ph isthmi men pou eiper autos en eautou
english_head: Certainly. Then the one, inasmuch as it is a whole, is in other and inasmuch as it is all its parts, it is in itself; and thus one must be both in itself and in other. It must. This being its nature, must not the one be both in motion and at rest? How is that? It is at rest, no doubt, if it is in itself; for being in one,

greek_head: ἐστιν ἐν γὰρ ἑνὶ ὂν καὶ ἐκ τούτου μὴ μεταβαῖνον ἐν τῷ αὐτῷ ἂν εἴη ἐν ἑαυτῷ Ἔστι γάρ Τὸ δέ γε ἐν τῷ αὐτῷ
greek_tokens: eimi en gar eis eimi kai ek outos mh metabainw en o autos an eaw en eautou eimi gar o de ge en o autos aei eimi isthmi dhpou anagkh aei eimi panu ge tis de o en eteros aei eimi ou o enantion anagkh mhdepote en tautazw eimi mhdepote de eimi en o autos mhde isthmi mh isthmi de kinew outws anagkh ara o eis autos te en eautou aei eimi kai en eteros aei kinew te kai isthmi fainw kai meis tautos ge deomai eimi autos eautou kai eteros
english_head: and not passing out from this, it is in the same, namely in itself. It is. But that which is always in the same, must always be at rest. Certainly. Well, then, must not, on the contrary, that which is always in other be never in the same, and being never in the same be not at rest, and being not at rest be in motion? True. Then the one, being always in itself and in other, must always be in motion and at rest. That is clear. And again, it must be the same with itself and other than itself,

greek_head: ἑαυτοῦ καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις ὡσαύτως ταὐτόν τε καὶ ἕτερον εἶναι εἴπερ καὶ τὰ πρόσθεν πέπονθεν Πῶς Πᾶν που πρὸς
greek_tokens: eautou kai o allos wsautws tautos te kai eteros eimi eiper kai o prosqen pasxw pws pas pou pros apas wde exis h tautos eimi h eteros h ean mh tautos eimi mhde eteros meros an eaw outos pros o outws exis h ws pros meros oloc an eaw fainw ara oun o eis autos eautou meros eimi oudamos oude ara ws pros meros autos eautou oloc an eaw pros eautou meros eimi ou gar oios
english_head: and likewise the same with all other things and other than they, if what we have said is true. How is that? Everything stands to everything in one of the following relations: it is either the same or other; or if neither the same or other, its relation is that of a part to a whole or of a whole to a part. Obviously. Now is the one a part of itself? By no means. Then it cannot, by being a part in relation to itself, be a whole in relation to itself, as a part of itself. No, that is impossible. Nor can it be other than itself.

greek_head: τε Ἀλλ' ἆρα ἕτερόν ἐστιν ἑνὸς τὸ ἕν Οὐ δῆτα Οὐδ' ἄρα ἑαυτοῦ γε ἕτερον ἂν εἴη Οὐ μέντοι Εἰ οὖν μήτε
greek_tokens: te alla ara eteros eimi eis o eis ou dhta oude ara eautou ge eteros an eaw ou mentoi ei oun mhte eteros mhte oloc mhte meros autos pros eautou eimi ou anagkh hdh tautos eimi autos eautou anagkh tis de o eterwqi eimi autos eautou en o autos eimi eautou ou anagkh autos eautou eteros eimi eiper kai eterwqi eimi egw dokew outws meis fainw exw o eis autos te en eautou eimi ama kai en eteros fainw gar eteros ara ws eoika
english_head: Certainly not. Then if it is neither other nor a part nor a whole in relation to itself, must it not therefore be the same with itself? It must. Well, must not that which is in another place than itself—the self being in the same place with itself—be other than itself, if it is to be in another place? I think so. Now we saw that this was the case with one, for it was in itself and in other at the same time. Yes, we saw that it was so. Then by this reasoning the one appears to be other than itself.

greek_head: εἴη ταύτῃ ἂν ἑαυτοῦ τὸ ἕν Ἔοικεν Τί οὖν εἴ τού τι ἕτερόν ἐστιν οὐχ ἑτέρου ὄντος ἕτερον ἔσται Ἀνάγκη
greek_tokens: eaw outos an eautou o eis eoika tis oun ei tis tis eteros eimi ou eteros eimi eteros eimi anagkh oukoun osos mh eis eimi apas eteros o eis kai o eis o mh eis pws de ou eteros ara an eaw o eis o allos eteros oraw dh autos te tautos kai o eteros ara ou enantios allhlwn pws de ou eimi oun eqelw tautos en o eteros h o eteros en tautazw pote eimi ou eqelw ei ara o eteros en o autos mhdepote eimi oudeis eimi o eimi en os eimi o eteros xronos oudeis
english_head: So it appears. Well then, if a thing is other than something, will it not be other than that which is other than it? Certainly. Are not all things which are not one, other than one, and the one other than the not one? Of course. Then the one would be other than the others. Yes, it is other. Consider; are not the absolute same and the absolute other opposites of one another? Of course. Then will the same ever be in the other, or the other in the same? No. Then if the other can never be in the same, there is no existing thing

greek_head: εἰ γὰρ ὁντινοῦν εἴη ἔν τῳ ἐκεῖνον ἂν τὸν χρόνον ἐν ταὐτῷ εἴη τὸ ἕτερον οὐχ οὕτως Οὕτως Ἐπειδὴ δ' οὐδέποτε
greek_tokens: ei gar ostis eaw en tis ekeinos an o xronos en tautazw eaw o eteros ou outws outws epei de oudepote en o autos eimi oudepote en tis o eimi an eaw o eteros alhqhs oute ara en o mh eis oute en o eis enihmi an o eteros ou gar oun ou ara o eteros ge an eaw o eis o mh eis oude o mh eis o eis eteros ou gar oude meis eautou ge eteros an eaw allhlwn mh metexw
english_head: in which the other is during any time; for if it were in anything during any time whatsoever, the other would be in the same, would it not? Yes, it would. But since the other is never in the same, it can never be in any existing thing. True. Then the other cannot be either in the not one or in the one. No, it cannot. Then not by reason of the other will the one be other than the not one or the not one other than the one. No. And surely they cannot by reason of themselves be other than one another, if they do not partake of the other.

greek_head: τοῦ ἑτέρου Πῶς γάρ Εἰ δὲ μήτε αὑτοῖς ἕτερά ἐστι μήτε τῷ ἑτέρῳ οὐ πάντῃ ἤδη ἂν ἐκφεύγοι τὸ μὴ ἕτερα εἶναι
greek_tokens: o eteros pws gar ei de mhte eautou eteros eimi mhte o eteros ou panth hdh an ekfeugw o mh eteros eimi allhlwn ekfeugw alla meis oude o eis ge metexw o mh eis ou gar an mh eis eimi alla ph an eis eimi alhqhs oude an ariqmos eaw ara o mh eis oude gar an outws mh eis eimi pantapasi ariqmos ge exw ou gar oun tis de o mh eis o eis ara morion eimi h kan outws metexw o eis o mh eis metexw ei ara panth o men eis eimi
english_head: Of course not. But if they are not other than one another either by reason of themselves or by reason of the other, will it not be quite impossible for them to be other than one another at all? Quite impossible. But neither can the not one partake of the one; for in that case they would not be not one, but would be one. True. Nor can the not one be a number; for in that case, too, since they would possess number, they would not be not one at all. No, they would not. Well, then, are the not one parts of the one? Or would the not one in that case also partake of the one? Yes, they would partake of it.

greek_head: τὰ δὲ μὴ ἕν οὔτ' ἂν μόριον τῶν μὴ ἓν τὸ ἓν εἴη οὔτε ὅλον ὡς μορίων οὔτε αὖ τὰ μὴ ἓν τοῦ ἑνὸς μόρια οὔτε ὅλα ὡς
greek_tokens: o de mh eis oute an morion o mh eis o eis eaw oute oloc ws morion oute au o mh eis o eis morion oute oloc ws morion o eis ou gar alla meis fhmi o mhte morion mhte oloc mhte eteros allhlwn tautos eimi allhlwn fhmi gar fhmi ara kai o eis pros o mh eis outws exw o autos eimi autos fhmi o eis ara ws eoika eteros te o allos eimi kai eautou kai tautos ekeinos te kai eautou kinduneuw fainw ek ge o logos
english_head: If, then, in every way the one is one and the not one are not one, the one cannot be a part of the not one, nor a whole of which the not one are parts, nor are the not one parts of the one, nor a whole of which the one is a part. No. But we said that things which are neither parts nor wholes of one another, nor other than one another, are the same as one another. Yes, we did. Shall we say, then, that since the relations of the one and the not one are such as we have described, the two are the same as one another? Yes, let us say that. The one, then, is, it appears, other than all other things and than itself, and is also the same as other things and as itself.

greek_head: Ἆρ' οὖν καὶ ὅμοιόν τε καὶ ἀνόμοιον ἑαυτῷ τε καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις Ἴσως Ἐπειδὴ γοῦν ἕτερον τῶν ἄλλων ἐφάνη
greek_tokens: ara oun kai omoios te kai anomoios eautou te kai o allos isos epei goun eteros o allos fainw kai allos pou eteros an ekeinos eaw tis meis oukoun outws eteros o allos wsper kai allos ekeinos kai oute mallon oute hsswn tis gar an ei ara mhte mallon mhte hsswn omoios nai oukoun h eteros eimi pasxw o allos kai allos ekeinos wsautws outos tautos an pasxw eien o te eis o allos kai allos o eis
english_head: That appears to be the result of our argument. Is it, then, also like and unlike itself and others? Perhaps. At any rate, since it was found to be other than others, the others must also be other than it. Of course. Then it is other than the others just as the others are other than it, neither more nor less? Certainly. And if neither more nor less, then in like degree? Yes. In so far as it is so affected as to be other than the others and the others are affected in the same way in relation to the one, to that degree the one will be affected

greek_head: Πῶς λέγεις Ὧδε ἕκαστον τῶν ὀνομάτων οὐκ ἐπί τινι καλεῖς Ἔγωγε Τί οὖν τὸ αὐτὸ ὄνομα εἴποις ἂν πλεονάκις
greek_tokens: pws legw wde ekastos o onoma ou epi tis kalew egw tis oun o autos onoma eipon an pleonakis h apac egw poteros oun ean men apac eipon ekeinos prosagoreuw ouper eimi onoma ean de pollakis ou ekeinos h ean apac ean pollakis tautos onoma fqegcis polus anagkh su tautos kai legw aei tis meis oukoun kai o eteros onoma eimi epi tis panu ge
english_head: in the same way as the others and the others in the same way as the one. What do you mean? I will explain. You give a particular name to a thing? Yes. Well, you can utter the same name once or more than once? Yes. And do you name that to which the name belongs when you utter it once, but not when you utter it many times? Or must you always mean the same thing when you utter the same name, whether once or repeatedly? The same thing, of course. The word other is the name of something, is it not? Certainly.

greek_head: Ὅταν ἄρα αὐτὸ φθέγγῃ ἐάντε ἅπαξ ἐάντε πολλάκις οὐκ ἐπ' ἄλλῳ οὐδὲ ἄλλο τι ὀνομάζεις ἢ ἐκεῖνο οὗπερ ἦν ὄνομα
greek_tokens: otan ara autos fqeggomai ean apac ean pollakis ou epi allos oude allos tis onomazw h ekeinos ouper eimi onoma anagkh otan dh legw ostis eteros men allos o eis eteros de o eis o allos dis o eteros eipon oudeis tis mallon epi allh alla epi ekeinos o fusis autos aei legw eis eimi onoma panu men oun h ara eteros o
english_head: Then when you utter it, whether once or many times, you apply it to nothing else, and you name nothing else, than that of which it is the name. Assuredly. Now when we say that the others are other than the one, and the one is other than the others, though we use the word other twice, we do not for all that apply it to anything else, but we always apply it to that nature of which it is the name. Certainly.

greek_head: ἄλλων τὸ ἓν καὶ τἆλλα τοῦ ἑνός κατ' αὐτὸ τὸ ἕτερον πεπονθέναι οὐκ ἄλλο ἀλλὰ τὸ αὐτὸ ἂν πεπονθὸς εἴη τὸ ἓν τοῖς
greek_tokens: allos o eis kai allos o eis kata autos o eteros pasxw ou allos alla o autos an pasxw eaw o eis o allos o de pou tautos pasxw omoios ou nai h dh o eis eteros o allos pasxw eimi kata autos outos apas apas omoios an eaw apas gar apas eteros eimi eoika alla meis o ge omoios o anomoios enantion nai oukoun kai o eteros o tautazw kai outos alla meis kai outos ge fainw ws ara o eis o
english_head: In so far as the one is other than the others and the others are other than the one, the one and the others are not in different states, but in the same state; but whatever is in the same state is like, is it not? Yes. Then in so far as the one is in the state of being other than the others, just so far everything is like all other things; for everything is other than all other things. So it appears. But the like is opposed to the unlike. Yes. And the other to the same. That is also true. But this, too, was shown, that the one is the same as the others.

greek_head: ἄλλοις ταὐτόν Ἐφάνη γάρ Τοὐναντίον δέ γε πάθος ἐστὶ τὸ εἶναι ταὐτὸν τοῖς ἄλλοις τῷ ἕτερον εἶναι τῶν ἄλλων
greek_tokens: allos tautos fainw gar enantion de ge paqos eimi o eimi tautos o allos o eteros eimi o allos panu ge h ge meis eteros omoios fainw nai h ara tautos anomoios eimi kata enantion paqos o omoiow paqos omoiow de pou o eteros nai anomoiwsis ara o tautos h ou enantion eimi o eteros
english_head: Yes, it was. And being the same as the others is the opposite of being other than the others. Certainly. In so far as it was other it was shown to be like. Yes. Then in so far as it is the same it will be unlike, since it has a quality which is the opposite of the quality which makes it like, for the other made it like. Yes. Then the same will make it unlike; otherwise the same will not be the opposite of the other.

greek_head: Ἔοικεν Ὅμοιον ἄρα καὶ ἀνόμοιον ἔσται τὸ ἓν τοῖς ἄλλοις ᾗ μὲν ἕτερον ὅμοιον ᾗ δὲ ταὐτόν ἀνόμοιον Ἔχει γὰρ οὖν
greek_tokens: eoika omoios ara kai anomoios eimi o eis o allos h men eteros omoios h de tautos anomoios exis gar oun dh ws eoika kai toioutos logos kai gar ode exis tis h tautos pasxw mh alloios pasxw mh alloios de pasxw mh anomoios mh anomoios de omoios eimi h de allos pasxw alloios alloios de eimi anomoios eimi alhqhs legw tautos te ara eimi o eis o allos kai ostis eteros eimi kata amfoteros kai kata ekateros omoios
english_head: So it appears. Then the one will be both like and unlike the others, like in so far as it is other, unlike in so far as it is the same. Yes, that sort of conclusion seems to be tenable. But there is another besides. What is it? In so far as it is in the same state, the one is not in another state, and not being in another state it is not unlike, and not being unlike it is like but in so far as it is in another state, it is of another sort, and being of another sort it is unlike. True. Then the one, because it is the same as the others and because it is other than the others, for both these reasons or for either of them would be both like and unlike the others.

greek_head: τε ἂν εἴη καὶ ἀνόμοιον τοῖς ἄλλοις Πάνυ γε Οὐκοῦν καὶ ἑαυτῷ ὡσαύτως ἐπείπερ ἕτερόν τε ἑαυτοῦ καὶ ταὐτὸν

english_head: Undoubtedly. But the present is inseparable from the one throughout its whole existence; for it always is now whenever it is. Of course. Always, then, the one is and is becoming younger than itself. So it appears. And is it or does it become for a longer time than itself, or for an equal time? For an equal time. But that which is or becomes for an equal time is of the same age. Of course. But that which is of the same age is neither older nor younger. No. Then the one, since it is and becomes for an equal time with itself, neither is nor becomes older or younger than itself. I agree. Well, then, is it or does it become older or younger than other things?

greek_head: Τόδε γε μὴν ἔχεις λέγειν ὅτι τὰ ἄλλα τοῦ ἑνός εἴπερ ἕτερά ἐστιν ἀλλὰ μὴ ἕτερον πλείω ἐστὶν ἑνός ἕτερον μὲν
greek_tokens: ode ge meis exis legw ostis o allos o eis eiper eteros eimi alla mh eteros plew eimi eis eteros men gar eimi eis an eimi eteros de eimi plew eis eimi kai plhqos an exw exw gar an plhqos de eimi ariqmew pleiwn an metexw h o eis pws de ou tis oun ariqmew fhmi o plew gignomai te kai gignomai proteros h o elasswn o elasswn o oligistos ara proteros outos
english_head: I cannot tell. But you can at any rate tell that the others, if they are others, not an other—plural, not singular—are more than one; for if they were an other, they would be one; but since they are others, they are more than one and have multitude. Yes, they have. And being a multitude, they would partake of a number greater than one. Of course. Well, which shall we say come and have come into being first, the greater or the smaller numbers? The smaller. Then the smallest comes into being first and that is the one, is it not?

greek_head: δ' ἔστι τὸ ἕν ἦ γάρ Ναί Πάντων ἄρα τὸ ἓν πρῶτον γέγονε τῶν ἀριθμὸν ἐχόντων ἔχει δὲ καὶ τἆλλα πάντα
greek_tokens: de eimi o eis eimi gar nai pas ara o eis proteros gignomai o ariqmos exw exis de kai allos pas ariqmos eiper allos kai mh allos eimi exis gar proteros de ge oimh gignomai proteros gignomai o de allos usteron o de usteron gignomai neos o proteros gignomai kai outws an eaw allos neos o eis o de eis presbus o allos eaw gar an tis de ode ara an eaw o eis para fus o eautou
english_head: Yes. The one, therefore, has come into being first of all things that have number; but all others also have number, if they are others and not an other. They have. And since it came into being first, it came into being, I suppose, before the others, and the others later; but things which have come into being later are younger than that which came into being before them and thus the other things would be younger than the one, and the one older than the other things. Yes, they would. Here is another question: Can the one have come into being contrary to its own nature, or is that impossible? It is impossible.

greek_head: γεγονός ἢ ἀδύνατον Ἀδύνατον Ἀλλὰ μὴν μέρη γε ἔχον ἐφάνη τὸ ἕν εἰ δὲ μέρη καὶ ἀρχὴν καὶ τελευτὴν καὶ μέσον
greek_tokens: gignomai h adunatos adunatos alla meis mera ge exw fainw o eis ei de mera kai arxh kai teleuth kai mesos nai oukoun pas proteros arxh gignomai kai autos o eis kai ekastos o allos kai meta o arxh kai allos pas mexri o telos tis meis kai meis morion ge fhmi outos eimi pas allos o oloc te kai eis autos de ekeinos ama o teleutaw gignomai eis te kai oloc fhmi gar teleuth de ge oimh ustatos gignomai outos
english_head: But surely the one was shown to have parts, a beginning, a middle, and an end. Yes. And the beginning of everything—of one and everything else alike—comes into being first, and after the beginning come all the other parts until the end arrives, do they not? Certainly. And we shall say also that all these others are parts of the whole and the one, and that it has become one and whole at the moment when the end arrives. Yes, we shall say that. The end, I imagine, comes into being last; and at that moment the one naturally comes into being;

greek_head: δ' ἅμα τὸ ἓν πέφυκε γίγνεσθαι ὥστ' εἴπερ ἀνάγκη αὐτὸ τὸ ἓν μὴ παρὰ φύσιν γίγνεσθαι ἅμα τελευτῇ ἂν γεγονὸς ὕστατον
greek_tokens: de ama o eis fuw gignomai wste eiper anagkh autos o eis mh para fus gignomai ama teleutaw an gignomai ustatos an o allos fuw eaw gignomai fainw neos ara o allos o eis eimi o de allos o eis presbus outws au egw fainw tis de dh arxh h allos meros otioun o eis h allos ostis ean meros eimi alla mh mera ou anagkaion eis eimi meros ge eimi anagkh oukoun o eis ama te o proteros gignomai gignomai an kai
english_head: so that if the absolute one cannot come into being contrary to its own nature, since it has come into being simultaneously with the end, its nature must be such that it comes into being after all the others. That is clear. Then the one is younger than the others and the others are older than the one. I think that is clear, too. Well, must not a beginning or any other part whatsoever of one or of anything else whatsoever, if it be a part, not parts, be one, since it is a part? It must.

greek_head: ἅμα τῷ δευτέρῳ καὶ οὐδενὸς ἀπολείπεται τῶν ἄλλων γιγνομένων ὅτιπερ ἂν προσγίγνηται ὁτῳοῦν ἕως ἂν πρὸς τὸ
greek_tokens: ama o deuteros kai oudeis apoleipw o allos gignomai oti an prosgignomai ostis ews an pros o esxatos dierxomai oloc eis gignomai oute meshs oute proteros oute esxatos oute allos oudeis apoleipw en o genesis alhqhs pas ara o allos o autos hlikia isxw o eis wste ei mh para fus fuw autos o eis oute proteros oute usteron o allos gignomai an eaw alla ama
english_head: Then the one would come into being simultaneously with the first part and with the second, and it is not wanting in any part which comes into being in addition to any part whatsoever which may precede it, until it reaches the end and becomes complete one; it will not be wanting in the middle, nor in the first, nor in the last, nor in any other part in the process of coming into being. True. Then one has the same age as all the others so that the absolute one, unless it is naturally contrary to nature, could not have come into being either before or after the others, but only simultaneously with them.

greek_head: καὶ κατὰ τοῦτον τὸν λόγον τὸ ἓν τῶν ἄλλων οὔτε πρεσβύτερον οὔτε νεώτερον ἂν εἴη οὐδὲ τἆλλα τοῦ ἑνός κατὰ δὲ τὸν
greek_tokens: kai kata outos o logos o eis o allos oute presbus oute neos an eaw oude allos o eis kata de o prosqen presbus te kai neos kai allos ekeinos wsautws panu men oun eimi men dh outws exw te kai gignomai alla tis au peri o gignomai autos presbus te kai neos o allos kai allos o eis kai mhte neos mhte presbus gignomai ara wsper peri o eimi outws kai peri o gignomai exis h eteros ou
english_head: And by this reasoning the one would be neither older nor younger than the others nor the others than the one, but of the same age; but by the previous reasoning the one would be both older and younger than the others, and likewise the others than the one. Certainly. In this state, then, it is and in this way it has come into being. But what about the one becoming older and younger than the others, and the others than the one, and becoming neither older nor younger? Is it the same with becoming as with being, or otherwise?

greek_head: ἔχω λέγειν Ἀλλ' ἐγὼ τοσόνδε γε εἰ καὶ ἔστιν πρεσβύτερον ἕτερον ἑτέρου γίγνεσθαί γε αὐτὸ πρεσβύτερον ἔτι ἢ ὡς τὸ
greek_tokens: exw legw alla egw tososde ge ei kai eimi presbus eteros eteros gignomai ge autos presbus eti h ws o proteros euqus gignomai diaferw o hlikia ou an eti dunamai oude au o neos eimi eti neos gignomai anisos gar isos prostiqhmi xronos te kai allos ostis isos poiew diaferw aei osos an o proteros diaferw pws gar ou ou ara o ge eimi o eis eimi gignomai
english_head: I cannot say. But I can say as much as this, that even if one thing be older than another, it cannot become older by any greater difference in age than that which existed at first, nor if younger can it become younger by any greater difference; for the addition of equals to unequals, whether in time or anything else whatsoever, makes the difference always equal to that which existed at first. Yes, of course. Then that which exists

greek_head: ἄν ποτε πρεσβύτερον οὐδὲ νεώτερον εἴπερ ἴσῳ διαφέρει ἀεὶ τὴν ἡλικίαν ἀλλ' ἔστι καὶ γέγονε πρεσβύτερον τὸ δὲ
greek_tokens: an pote presbus oude neos eiper isos diaferw aei o hlikia alla eimi kai gignomai presbus o de neos gignomai de ou alhqhs kai o eis ara eimi o allos eimi oute presbus pote oute neos gignomai ou gar oun oraw de ei ode presbus kai neos gignomai ph dh h o te eis o allos fainw presbus kai allos o eis tis oun otan o eis o allos presbus eimi plew pou xronos gignomai h o
english_head: can never become older or younger than that which exists, if the difference in age is always the same; but it is and has become older, and the other is and has become younger, but it does not become so. True. And the one, since it exists, never becomes either older or younger than the other things. No, it does not. But see whether they become older and younger in this way. In what way? Because the one was found to be older than the others, and the others than the one. What then? When the one is older than the others,

greek_head: ἄλλα Ναί Πάλιν δὴ σκόπει ἐὰν πλέονι καὶ ἐλάττονι χρόνῳ προστιθῶμεν τὸν ἴσον χρόνον ἆρα τῷ ἴσῳ μορίῳ
greek_tokens: allos nai palin dh skopaw ean pleiwn kai elasswn xronos prostiqhmi o isos xronos ara o isos morion diaferw o pleos o elasswn h mikros mikros ou ara eimi oti o proteros eimi pros allos hlikia diaferw o eis outos kai eis o epeita alla isos lambanw xronos o allos elasswn aei o hlikia diaferw autos h proteros h ou nai oukoun o ge elasswn diaferw
english_head: it has come into being a longer time than the others. Yes. Then consider again. If we add an equal to a greater and to a less time, will the greater differ from the less by the same or by a smaller fraction? By a smaller fraction. Then the proportional difference in age which existed originally between the one and the others will not continue afterwards, but if an equal time be added to the one and the others, the difference in their ages will constantly diminish, will it not?

greek_head: ἡλικίᾳ πρός τι ἢ πρότερον νεώτερον γίγνοιτ' ἂν ἢ ἐν τῷ πρόσθεν πρὸς ἐκεῖνα πρὸς ἃ ἦν πρεσβύτερον πρότερον
greek_tokens: hlikia pros tis h proteros neos gignomai an h en o prosqen pros ekeinos pros o eimi presbus proteros neos ei de ekeinos neos ou ekeinos au o allos pros o eis presbus h proteros panu ge o men neos ara gignomai presbus gignomai pros o proteros gignomai te kai presbus eimi eimi de oudepote presbus alla gignomai aei ekeinos presbus ekeinos men gar epi o neos epididwmi o de epi o presbus
english_head: Yes. And that which differs less in age from something than before becomes younger than before in relation to those things than which it formerly was older? Yes, it becomes younger. But if the one becomes younger, must not those other things in turn become older than formerly in relation to the one? Certainly. Then that which came into being later, becomes older in relation to the older, which came into being earlier; yet it never is older, but is always becoming older; for the latter always tends towards being younger,

greek_head: τὸ δ' αὖ πρεσβύτερον τοῦ νεωτέρου νεώτερον γίγνεται ὡσαύτως ἰόντε γὰρ αὐτοῖν εἰς τὸ ἐναντίον τὸ ἐναντίον
greek_tokens: o de au presbus o neos neos gignomai wsautws eimi gar autos eis o enantion o enantion allhlwn gignomai o men neos presbus o presbus o de presbus neos o neos gignomai de ou an oios te eimi ei gar gignomai ou an eti gignomai alla eien an nun de gignomai men presbus allhlwn kai neos o men eis o allos neos gignomai ostis presbus fainw eimi kai proteros
english_head: and the former towards being older. And conversely the older becomes in the same way younger than the younger. For as they are moving in opposite directions, they are becoming the opposites of one another, the younger older than the older, and the older younger than the younger; but they cannot finish the process of becoming; for if they finished the process of becoming, they would no longer be becoming, they would be. But as the case is, they become older and younger than one another—the one becomes younger than the others, because, as we saw, it is older and came into being earlier,

greek_head: γεγονός τὰ δὲ ἄλλα τοῦ ἑνὸς πρεσβύτερα ὅτι ὕστερα γέγονε κατὰ δὲ τὸν αὐτὸν λόγον καὶ τἆλλα οὕτω πρὸς
greek_tokens: gignomai o de allos o eis presbus ostis usteron gignomai kata de o autos logos kai allos outws pros o eis isxw epei autos presbus fainw kai proteros gignomai fainw gar oun outws oukoun h men oudeis eteros eteros presbus gignomai oude neos kata o isos ariqmos allhlwn aei diaferw oute o eis o allos presbus gignomai an oude neos oute allos o eis h de allos aei morion diaferw anagkh
english_head: and the others are becoming older than the one, because they came into being later. By the same reasoning the others stand in the same relation to the one, since they were seen to be older than the one and to have come into being earlier. Yes, that is clear. Then from the point of view that no one thing becomes older or younger than another, inasmuch as they always differ by an equal number, the one cannot become older or younger than the others, nor the others than the one; but in so far as that which comes into being earlier must always differ by a different proportional part from that which comes into being later,

greek_head: τὰ πρότερα τῶν ὑστέρων γενόμενα καὶ τὰ ὕστερα τῶν προτέρων ταύτῃ δὴ ἀνάγκη πρεσβύτερά τε καὶ νεώτερα
greek_tokens: o proteros o usteron gignomai kai o usteron o proteros outos dh anagkh presbus te kai neos allhlwn gignomai o te allos o eis kai o eis o allos panu men oun kata dh pas outos o eis autos te eautou kai o allos presbus kai neos eimi te kai gignomai kai oute presbus oute neos oute eimi oute gignomai oute eautou oute o allos pantelhs men oun epei de xronos metexw o eis kai o
english_head: and vice versa—from this point of view the one and the others must necessarily become both older and younger than one another, must they not? Certainly. For all these reasons, then, the one both is and becomes both older and younger than both itself and the others, and neither is nor becomes either older or younger than either itself or the others. Perfectly true. But since the one partakes of time and can become older and younger,

greek_head: πρεσβύτερόν τε καὶ νεώτερον γίγνεσθαι ἆρ' οὐκ ἀνάγκη καὶ τοῦ ποτὲ μετέχειν καὶ τοῦ ἔπειτα καὶ τοῦ νῦν εἴπερ χρόνου
greek_tokens: presbus te kai neos gignomai ara ou anagkh kai o pote metexw kai o epeita kai o nun eiper xronos metexw anagkh eimi ara o eis kai eimi kai eimi kai gignomai kai gignomai kai gignomai tis meis kai eaw an tis ekeinos kai ekeinos kai eimi kai eimi kai eimi panu ge kai episthmh dh eaw an autos kai doca kai aisqhsis eiper kai nun egw peri autos pas outos prassw orqos legw kai onoma dh kai logos eimi autos kai
english_head: must it not also partake of the past, the future, and the present? It must. Then the one was and is and will be and was becoming and is becoming and will become. Certainly. And there would be and was and is and will be something which is in relation to it and belongs to it? Certainly. And there would be knowledge and opinion and perception of it; there must be, if we are now carrying on all this discussion about it. You are right. And it has a name and definition, is named and defined,

greek_head: ὀνομάζεται καὶ λέγεται καὶ ὅσαπερ καὶ περὶ τὰ ἄλλα τῶν τοιούτων τυγχάνει ὄντα καὶ περὶ τὸ ἓν ἔστιν Παντελῶς
greek_tokens: onomazw kai legw kai osos kai peri o allos o toioutos tugxanw eimi kai peri o eis eimi pantelhs men oun exis outws eti dh o tritos legw o eis ei eimi oios dierxomai ara ou anagkh autos eis te eimi kai polus kai mhte eis mhte polus kai metexw xronos ostis men eimi eis ousia metexw pote ostis de ou eimi mh metexw au pote ousia anagkh ara oun oste metexw oios te eimi tote mh metexw h oste mh metexw metexw ou oios te en allos ara xronos metexw kai en allos ou metexw outws gar an monos o autos metexw te kai ou metexw
english_head: and all the similar attributes which pertain to other things pertain also to the one. That is perfectly true. Let us discuss the matter once more and for the third time. If the one is such as we have described it, being both one and many and neither one nor many, and partakes of time, must it not, because one is, sometimes partake of being, and again because one is not, sometimes not partake of being? Yes, it must. And can one, when it partakes of being, not partake of it, or partake of it when it does not partake of it? No, it cannot. Then it partakes at one time and does not partake at another; for that is the only way in which it can partake and not partake of the same thing.

greek_head: Ὀρθῶς Οὐκοῦν ἔστι καὶ οὗτος χρόνος ὅτε μεταλαμβάνει τοῦ εἶναι καὶ ὅτε ἀπαλλάττεται αὐτοῦ ἢ πῶς οἷόν τε ἔσται
greek_tokens: orqos oukoun eimi kai outos xronos oste metalambanw o eimi kai oste apallassw autos h pws oios te eimi tote men exw o autos tote de mh exw ean mh pote kai lambanw autos kai afia oudamos o dh ousia metalambanw ara ge ou gignomai kalew egw o de apallassw ousia ara ou apollumi kai panu ge o eis dh ws eoika lambanw te kai afihmi ousia gignomai
english_head: True. And is there not also a time when it assumes being and when it gives it up? How can it sometimes have and sometimes not have the same thing, unless it receives it at some time and again loses it? There is no other way at all. But would you not say that receiving existence is generation or becoming? Yes. And losing existence is destruction? Certainly. The one, then, as it appears, since it receives and loses existence, is generated and destroyed.

greek_head: τε καὶ ἀπόλλυται Ἀνάγκη Ἓν δὲ καὶ πολλὰ ὂν καὶ γιγνόμενον καὶ ἀπολλύμενον ἆρ' οὐχ ὅταν μὲν γίγνηται ἕν
greek_tokens: te kai apollumi anagkh eis de kai polus eimi kai gignomai kai apollumi ara ou otan men gignomai eis o polus eimi apollumi otan de polus o eis eimi apollumi panu ge eis de gignomai kai polus ara ou anagkh diakrinw te kai sugkrinw polus ge kai meis anomoios ge kai omoios otan gignomai omoiow te kai anomoiow nai kai otan megas kai elasswn kai isos aucanw te kai fqiw kai isow outws
english_head: Inevitably. And being one and many and being generated and destroyed, when it becomes one its existence as many is destroyed, and when it becomes many its existence as one is destroyed, is it not? Certainly. And in becoming one and many, must it not be separated and combined? Inevitably. And when it becomes like and unlike, it must be assimilated and dissimilated? Yes. And when it becomes greater and smaller and equal, it must be increased and diminished and equalized?

greek_head: Ὅταν δὲ κινούμενόν τε ἵστηται καὶ ὅταν ἑστὸς ἐπὶ τὸ κινεῖσθαι μεταβάλλῃ δεῖ δήπου αὐτό γε μηδ' ἐν ἑνὶ χρόνῳ
greek_tokens: otan de kinew te isthmi kai otan isthmi epi o kinew metaballw deomai dhpou autos ge mhde en eis xronos eimi pws dh isthmi te proteros usteron kinew kai proteros kinew usteron isthmi aneu men o metaballw ou oios te eimi outos pasxw pws gar xronos de ge oudeis eimi en os tis oios te ama mhte kinew mhte isthmi ou gar oun alla oude meis metaballw aneu o metaballw ou eoika pote oun metaballw oute gar isthmi eimi oute kinew metaballw oute en
english_head: Yes. And when being in motion it comes to rest, and when being at rest it changes to motion, it must itself be in no time at all. How is that? It is impossible for it to be previously at rest and afterwards in motion, or previously in motion and afterwards at rest, without changing. Of course. And there is no time in which anything can be at once neither in motion nor at rest. No, there is none. And certainly it cannot change without changing. I should say not. Then when does it change? For it does not change when it is at rest or when it is in motion or when it is in time.

greek_head: χρόνῳ ὄν Οὐ γὰρ οὖν Ἆρ' οὖν ἔστι τὸ ἄτοπον τοῦτο ἐν ᾧ τότ' ἂν εἴη ὅτε μεταβάλλει Τὸ ποῖον δή Τὸ
greek_tokens: xronos eimi ou gar oun ara oun eimi o atopos outos en os tote an eaw oste metaballw o poios dh o ecaifnhs o gar ecaifnhs toiosde tis eoika shmainw ws ek ekeinos metaballw eis ekateros ou gar ek ge o isthmi isthmi eti metaballw oude ek o kinhsis kinew eti metaballw alla o ecaifnhs outos fusis atopos tis egkaqhmai metacu o kinhsis te kai stasis
english_head: No, it does not. Does this strange thing, then, exist, in which it would be at the moment when it changes? What sort of thing is that? The instant. For the instant seems to indicate a something from which there is a change in one direction or the other. For it does not change from rest while it is still at rest, nor from motion while it is still moving; but there is this strange instantaneous nature, something interposed between

greek_head: ἐν χρόνῳ οὐδενὶ οὖσα καὶ εἰς ταύτην δὴ καὶ ἐκ ταύτης τό τε κινούμενον μεταβάλλει ἐπὶ τὸ ἑστάναι καὶ τὸ ἑστὸς ἐπὶ τὸ
greek_tokens: en xronos oudeis eimi kai eis outos dh kai ek outos o te kinew metaballw epi o isthmi kai o isthmi epi o kinew kinduneuw kai o eis dh eiper isthmi te kai kinew metaballw an epi ekateros monos gar an outws amfoteros poiew metaballw de ecaifnhs metaballw kai oste metaballw en oudeis xronos an eaw oude kinew an tote oude an isthmi ou gar ara oun outws kai pros o allos metabolh exis otan ek o eimi eis o apollumi
english_head: motion and rest, not existing in any time, and into this and out from this that which is in motion changes into rest and that which is at rest changes into motion. Yes, that must be so. Then the one, if it is at rest and in motion, must change in each direction; for that is the only way in which it can do both. But in changing, it changes instantaneously, and when it changes it can be in no time, and at that instant it will be neither in motion nor at rest. No. And will the case not be the same in relation to other changes? When it changes from being to destruction

greek_head: μεταβάλλῃ ἢ ἐκ τοῦ μὴ εἶναι εἰς τὸ γίγνεσθαι μεταξύ τινων τότε γίγνεται κινήσεών τε καὶ στάσεων καὶ οὔτε
greek_tokens: metaballw h ek o mh eimi eis o gignomai metacu tis tote gignomai kinhsis te kai stasis kai oute eimi tote oute ou eimi oute gignomai oute apollumi eoika goun kata dh o autos logos kai ek eis epi polus eimi kai ek polus epi eis oute eis eimi oute polus oute diakrinw oute sugkrinw kai ek omoios epi anomoios kai ek anomoios epi omoios eimi oute omoios oute anomoios oute omoiow oute anomoiow kai
english_head: or from not being to becoming, does it not pass into an intermediate stage between certain forms of motion and rest, so that it neither is nor is not, neither comes into being nor is destroyed? Yes, so it appears. And on the same principle, when it passes from one to many or from many to one, it is neither one nor many, is neither in a process of separation nor in one of combination. And in passing from like to unlike or from unlike to like, it is neither like nor unlike, neither in a process of assimilation nor in one of dissimilation;

greek_head: ἐκ σμικροῦ ἐπὶ μέγα καὶ ἐπὶ ἴσον καὶ εἰς τὰ ἐναντία ἰὸν οὔτε σμικρὸν οὔτε μέγα οὔτε ἴσον οὔτε αὐξανόμενον

english_head: and in passing from small to great and to equal and vice versa, it is neither small nor great nor equal, neither in a process of increase, nor of diminution, nor of equality. Apparently not. All this, then, would happen to the one, if the one exists. Yes, certainly. Must we not consider what is likely to happen to the other things, if the one exists? We must. Shall we tell, then, what must happen to the things other than one, if one exists? Let us do so. Well, since they are other than the one, the other things are not the one for if they were, they would not be other than the one. True.

english_head: And yet surely the others are not altogether deprived of the one, but they partake of it in a certain way. In what way? Yes, we affirm that they do. But the whole must be one composed of many and of this the parts are parts. For each of the parts must be a part, not of many, but of a whole. How is that? If anything is a part of many, and is itself one of the many, it will be a part of itself,

english_head: the word each implies that it is one, separated from the rest, and existing by itself; otherwise it will not be each. True. Yes, it is impossible. And both the whole and the part must necessarily participate in the one; for the one will be a whole of which the parts are parts, and again each individual one which is a part of a whole will be a part of the whole. Yes.

Inasmuch as they are all by their own nature unlimited, they are all in that respect affected in the same way. Certainly. And inasmuch as they are so affected as to be both limited and limitless, they are affected by affections which are the opposites of one another.

Yes, we do know. english_head: Certainly. But that is inadmissible. It certainly is. Then the one must possess likeness to itself. It must. And neither is the one equal to the others; for if it were equal, then it would both be and be like them in respect to equality, both of which are impossible, if one does not exist. Yes, they are impossible. And since it is not equal to the others, they cannot be equal to it, can they? Certainly not. And things which are not equal are unequal, are they not? Yes. And things which are unequal are unequal to something which is unequal to them? Of course. Then the one partakes of inequality, in respect to which the others are unequal to it?

greek_head: πρὸς ἣν τἆλλα αὐτῷ ἐστιν ἄνισα Μετέχει Ἀλλὰ μέντοι ἀνισότητός γε ἐστὶ μέγεθός τε καὶ σμικρότης Ἔστι γάρ

english_head: Yes, it does. But greatness and smallness are constituents of inequality. Yes. Then the one, such as we are discussing, possesses greatness and smallness? So it appears. Now surely greatness and smallness always keep apart from one another. Certainly. Then there is always something between them. There is. Can you think of anything between them except equality? No, only equality. Then anything which has greatness and smallness has also equality, which is between the two. That is clear.

It must be in such conditions as we have been saying; for if it were not, we should not be speaking the truth in saying that the one is not. english_head: we necessarily assert that we say that which is. Necessarily. Then, as it appears, the non-existent one exists. For if it is not non-existent, but gives up something of being to not-being, then it will be existent. Certainly. Then if it does not exist and is to continue to be non-existent, it must have the existence of not-being as a bond, just as being has the non-existence of not-being, in order to attain its perfect existence. For in this way the existence of the existent and the non-existence of the non-existent would be best assured, when the existent partakes of the existence of being existent and of the non-existence of not being non-existent,

Very true. Then since the existent partakes of non-existence and the non-existent of existence, the one, since it does not exist, necessarily partakes of existence to attain non-existence. Yes, necessarily. Clearly, then, the one, if it does not exist, has existence. Clearly. And non-existence also, if it does not exist. Of course. Well, can anything which is in a certain condition be not in that condition without changing from it? No, it cannot. Then everything of that sort—if a thing is and is not in a given condition—signifies a change.

english_head: Of course. But change is motion; we agree to that? It is motion. And did we not see that the one is and is not? Yes. Then we see that it both is and is not in a given condition. So it appears. And we have seen that the non-existent one has motion, since it changes from being to not-being. There is not much doubt of that. But if it is nowhere among existing things—and it is nowhere, if it does not exist—it cannot move from any place to another. Of course not. Then its motion cannot be change of place. No, it cannot.

Quite right. But if it neither changes into something else,

No. Then in so far as the non-existent one moves, it changes, and in so far as it does not move, it does not change. True. Then the non-existent one both changes and does not change. So it appears. But that which has no participation in it can neither receive it nor lose it. Of course not. Then the one, since it does not exist in any way, cannot possess or lose or share in existence at all. That is reasonable. Then the non-existent one neither perishes nor comes into being, since it participates in no way in existence. No; that is clear. Then it is not changed in nature at all;

Thus we shall say again that the non-existent one is neither at rest nor in motion. No, neither. Nor can anything which exists pertain to it for the moment it partook of anything which exists it would partake of existence.

No. Well, then, will the notions of that or to that or some, or this or of this

No. Then the non-existent one has no state or condition whatsoever. It appears to have none whatsoever. Let us then discuss further what happens to the other things, if the one does not exist. Or do you not regard the words other and different as synonymous?

Then if the others are to be others, there must be something of which they will be others. Yes, there must be. Now what can that be? For they cannot be others of the one, if it does not exist. No. Then they are others of each other; for they have no alternative, except to be others of nothing. True. They are each, then, others of each other, in groups; for they cannot be so one at a time, if one does not exist.

Very true. Such masses of others would be others of each other, if others exist and one does not exist. Certainly. There will, then, be many masses, each of which appears to be one, but is not one, if one does not exist? Yes.

Now anything of that sort, if seen from a distance and dimly, must appear to be one,

english_head: if the many exist and the one does not. Very true. Let us, then, go back once more to the beginning and tell the consequences, if the others exist and the one does not. Let us do so. Well, the others will not be one? Of course not. Nor will they be many for if they were many, one would be contained in them. And if none of them is one, they are all nothing, so that they cannot be many. True. If one is not contained in the others, the others are neither many nor one.

english_head: No. And they do not even appear to be one or many. Why is that? Because the others have no communion in any way whatsoever with anything which is non-existent, and nothing that is non-existent pertains to any of the others, for things that are non-existent have no parts. True. Nor is there any opinion or appearance of the non-existent in connection with the others, nor is the non-existent conceived of in any way whatsoever as related to the others. english_head: True. Then if we were to say in a word, if the one is not, nothing is, should we be right? Most assuredly. Then let us say that, and we may add, as it appears, that whether the one is or is not, the one and the others in relation to themselves and to each other all in every way are and are not and appear and do not appear. Very true.
